evidence: Action vocabulary is defined in `goseg/protocol/actionids/actionids.go` but conformance checks in `goseg/protocol/conformance/fixtures_test.go` use `groundseg/protocol/actions` symbols, creating multiple package-level authorities for the same namespace/action bindings.

`goseg/protocol/contracts/protocol_contracts.go` re-wraps `actionids` constants into contract-local types and IDs, while `goseg/uploadsvc/actions_test.go` validates upload action support via uploadsvc-local action APIs; this spreads canonical binding ownership across protocol, contracts, and service layers.

Cross-module alignment is enforced via test assertions in multiple packages (conformance/contracts/uploadsvc), indicating boundary integrity depends on distributed checks rather than a single authoritative manifest boundary.

Contract governance ownership and lifecycle metadata are string literals in multiple families (`OwnerModule`, `SinceVersion` in both `protocol_contracts.go` and `startram_contracts.go`), increasing drift risk and weakening explicit ownership boundaries.

Public contract-ID naming policy is inconsistent: upload has explicit backward-compatible aliases in `protocol_contracts.go`, while other families/actions use different naming exposure patterns, forcing mixed usage conventions in tests (`family_specs_test.go`).

Descriptor/governance declaration patterns are repeated per contract entry with no shared higher-level declaration model, so adding a new action likely requires edits in several files and conventions.

`main.go` and `startup_orchestrator.go` both wire core behavior by importing many concrete subsystems directly (config, system, routines, docker orchestration, startup deps), concentrating dependency fan-in at top-level orchestration.

`docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go` exposes a very large cross-domain runtime surface (context, image, volume, wireguard, minio, startup, command ops), indicating broad dependency coupling instead of narrower domain-specific dependency boundaries.

`importer/importer.go` depends directly on many unrelated domains (auth, docker orchestration, shipcreator, shipcleanup, transition, workflow orchestration), increasing ripple risk for routine changes.

`shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go` repeatedly performs resolve/validate/rebind steps (`resolveWorkflowRuntime`, `newWorkflowRuntime`, then delegate), so boundary handoffs are scattered across many entrypoints rather than centralized once.
`importer/importer.go` repeats runtime assembly/validation patterns across init/upload/workflow/publish scopes, creating seam choreography duplication and increasing integration complexity.

`startup_orchestrator.go` contains multiple layered runtime structs and with-default merge wrappers (`startupInitRuntimeWithDefaults`, `startupRuntimeWithDefaults`, `startBackgroundServicesRuntimeWithDefaults`) that replicate boundary setup logic.
`routines/system/system.go` uses `time.After(interval)` inside an unbounded loop in `runRoutineWithContext`, creating a new timer allocation every cycle instead of reusing a ticker.
`routines/logstream/logs.go` calls `regexp.MatchString` for each file in `runLogCleanupCycle`, recompiling the regex repeatedly inside the loop instead of compiling once.
These hot-path allocation patterns are avoidable and reduce internal precision/efficiency of routine loops.
All four files contain near-identical two-line package comments with a similar templated pattern ('Package <name> ... and ...').
Comments summarize broadly but do not capture package-specific constraints, invariants, or boundary intent, which is a common low-signal boilerplate pattern.
ai_generated_debt.issues_preventing_higher_score: Package docs read as formulaic summaries and miss differentiating technical intent, increasing risk of stale or low-value documentation.

evidence: No deprecated markers, TODO migration notes, temporary compatibility wording, or old/new API coexistence indicators are present in these four files.
The files are package docs only, so migration state cannot be fully validated from this batch’s scope.
incomplete_migration.issues_preventing_higher_score: Evidence is limited to doc stubs; absence of migration markers here does not prove migration completion in implementation files.

evidence: `goseg/protocol/conformance/fixtures_test.go` validates fixture shape/presence and one unknown-action negative case, but does not assert governance metadata policy (owner vocabulary, version format/progression, compatibility semantics).

`goseg/protocol/contracts/family_specs_test.go` heavily tests helper behavior with handcrafted specs, but does not provide end-to-end assertions that README-declared governance/quality-gate intent stays aligned with live contract catalogs in `protocol_contracts.go` and `startram_contracts.go`.

goseg/docker/orchestration/subsystem/subsystem_events.go reads from `messages` and `errs` channels without `ok` checks (lines 183, 196), so closed channels can keep selecting immediately and repeatedly process zero-values/`nil` in a loop.
goseg/main.go parseStartupOptions has no default branch in its arg switch (lines 531-550), so unknown CLI flags are silently ignored instead of being rejected.
Most runtime seams are strongly typed (for example goseg/system/wifi_runtime_api.go validates required callbacks before use).
Several exported APIs still rely on function-typed parameters without full nil guarding (for example goseg/shipworkflow/lifecycle.go PersistUrbitConfig calls `persistFn` without checking it first).
type_safety.issues_preventing_higher_score: Function-typed dependency parameters remain partially unchecked, leaving panic paths that static typing does not prevent.

evidence: goseg/shipworkflow/lifecycle.go PersistUrbitConfig validates `mutate` but not `persistFn` (lines 69-74), so caller-visible contract is incomplete and can panic on nil dependency.

goseg/shipworkflow/lifecycle.go PollWithTimeout directly calls `time.NewTicker(interval)` (line 77) without rejecting zero/negative intervals, so a public helper can panic rather than return an error.
goseg/importer/importer.go OpenUploadEndpoint collapses unauthorized outcomes to generic strings (`token mismatch` / `upload token id is not authorized`, lines 437-440).

goseg/importer/importer.go validateUploadRequest for the same token domain returns wrapped causes (`upload token validation failed: %w`, line 731), producing different diagnostic depth for sibling auth paths.
goseg/config/config.go performs package init (`init` at lines 230-232) that computes runtime context immediately, including environment/base-path and docker-directory defaults.
goseg/defaults/defaults.go DockerData performs filesystem probes (`os.Stat`) used by config runtime defaults (line 111 in config.go), so package import order and test timing influence startup state.
goseg/main.go uses package `init` to construct web FS/file server globals (lines 301-309), adding another import-time startup dependency surface.
goseg/config/config_events.go keeps multiple naming eras for the same API (`StartConfigEventLoop*`, `StartConfEventLoop*`, `ConfChannel`).
goseg/protocol/contracts/protocol_contracts.go keeps backward-compatibility aliases (`UploadActionOpenEndpoint`, `UploadActionReset`) alongside new canonical names.
convention_outlier.issues_preventing_higher_score: Legacy alias layering is still visible in exported surfaces and keeps naming conventions less predictable across packages.

evidence: goseg/config/config_events.go exposes overlapping event-loop entrypoints with different behavior contracts: some return errors (`StartConfigEventLoop*`), while `StartConfigEventLoopFromLegacyChannel`/`ConfChannel` swallow startup errors and only log.

goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator.go depends on the error-returning API (`StartConfigEventLoopFn` line 219), creating mixed expectations for callers depending on which exported entrypoint they use.

Upload-session authorization is implemented in both OpenUploadEndpoint and validateUploadRequest in goseg/importer/importer.go, but these sibling paths emit different unauthorized error semantics/messages.
No explicit hard bypasses were observed in this batch scope, but authorization decision and reporting behavior is not fully centralized.
authorization_consistency.issues_preventing_higher_score: Authorization checks exist, but duplicated decision/reporting paths create inconsistent caller-observable behavior.

findings.summary: Canonical protocol action identity is split across multiple modules, creating a high-coupling change surface.
evidence: `actionids.go` defines raw namespace/action tokens.
`protocol_contracts.go` redefines those tokens as contract-layer typed constants and derives contract IDs from them.
`fixtures_test.go` validates bindings against `protocol/actions` package symbols instead of `actionids` or contract IDs.
`actions_test.go` checks upload action coverage via uploadsvc action APIs, adding another representation boundary.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a single protocol action manifest package that owns namespace/action/contract-ID tuples and have contracts, conformance fixtures, and uploadsvc mapping consume that manifest directly; keep thin typed adapters only where required.

findings.summary: Governance ownership/version metadata is duplicated as free-form strings across contract families.
evidence: `protocol_contracts.go` repeats `OwnerModule` and `SinceVersion` literals per entry (e.g., `uploadsvc`, `system/wifi`, `2026.03.02`).
`startram_contracts.go` duplicates the same pattern with separate literals (`startram`, `2026.01.20`).
`family_specs_test.go` hardcodes matching ownership semantics in test fixture specs, increasing synchronization burden.
findings.suggestion: Define typed governance owners and version constants in one shared governance declaration layer, then reference those constants from all family specs and tests to prevent drift.

findings.summary: Backward-compatibility alias strategy is applied unevenly, producing an inconsistent top-level contract surface.
evidence: `protocol_contracts.go` defines upload backward-compatible aliases (`UploadActionOpenEndpoint`, `UploadActionReset`) but no equivalent alias policy for C2C IDs.
`family_specs_test.go` mixes alias-era naming (`UploadActionOpenEndpoint`) with canonical naming (`C2CConnectAction`), reflecting inconsistent public naming conventions.
findings.suggestion: Codify one alias/deprecation policy for all contract families (either canonical-only or uniform alias coverage) and enforce it with a dedicated naming-policy test.

findings.summary: Core orchestration layers depend on too many concrete packages, making config/orchestration a change-amplifying dependency hub.
evidence: `main.go` directly wires connectivity, startup bootstrap, server startup, and system mode behavior with concrete package calls rather than narrow interfaces.
`startup_orchestrator.go` imports and coordinates auth/config/router/importer/rectify/routines/system/startram directly in one place.
`runtime_ops.go` defines a wide runtime surface spanning unrelated responsibilities, and `importer.go` consumes broad orchestration/runtime dependencies.
findings.suggestion: Introduce bounded dependency ports per domain (startup, workflow, container lifecycle, config snapshots), then compose adapters once at the process root; split current runtime bundles into smaller capability interfaces to reduce fan-in and blast radius.

findings.summary: Runtime validation and default-resolution are repeatedly re-executed across seams, creating glue-code entropy at module handoff points.
evidence: `operations_runtime.go` has many functions that first resolve runtime then immediately delegate, duplicating seam setup logic across APIs.
`importer.go` repeats scope-specific validation and runtime composition (init/upload/workflow/publish) with similar control flow.
`startup_orchestrator.go` and `wifi_runtime_api.go` repeat default-merge/prepare patterns, indicating no single obvious seam boundary owner.
findings.suggestion: Centralize runtime resolution to one constructor per subsystem and pass resolved runtime down call chains; remove per-function resolve boilerplate and keep only capability-specific adapters near module boundaries.

findings.summary: Long-running loops allocate transient timers/regex objects repeatedly instead of reusing prebuilt primitives.
evidence: `runRoutineWithContext` uses `time.After(interval)` in each iteration, producing recurring timer allocations.
`runLogCleanupCycle` calls `regexp.MatchString` in the file loop, forcing per-file regexp compilation.
findings.suggestion: Replace `time.After` loop waits with a reused `time.Ticker` (or reusable timer), and precompile the logfile regex once (package var or function-local compiled regexp) before iterating files.

findings.summary: Package docs use repeated template phrasing with low domain-specific signal.
evidence: All comments follow the same formula ('Package X <verb phrase>, including ... and ...').
None of the docs describe why boundaries exist, key constraints, or non-obvious behavior.
findings.suggestion: Rewrite each package comment with concrete scope statements: what the package owns, what it explicitly does not own, and one key invariant or integration boundary per package.

findings.summary: Single-sentence docs bundle multiple concerns, weakening package contract clarity.
evidence: Comments enumerate several capabilities without clarifying primary responsibility or boundary edges.
Readers cannot infer which responsibilities are core vs incidental from the current wording.
findings.suggestion: Split each package doc into 2-4 short sentences: primary responsibility first, then explicit boundary notes and key collaborator packages.

findings.summary: The config package mixes bootstrap orchestration, runtime context wiring, and config-state ownership.
evidence: `initializeConfig` in `bootstrap.go` handles process startup sequencing and environment-derived setup, including debug-mode argument parsing and base-path initialization.

`config.go` also owns runtime context defaults (`basePathDefault`, `architectureDefault`, `dockerDirDefault`) and mutable config snapshots/state APIs (`SetStartramConfig`, settings snapshot structs), combining execution-context concerns with config-domain state.
findings.suggestion: Split ownership into explicit layers: keep persistent config/state APIs in `config`, move process/runtime bootstrap and runtime-context derivation into a dedicated bootstrap/runtime package, and leave `config.Initialize` as a thin composition point.

findings.summary: Top-level `system` files duplicate subpackage APIs via pass-through shims, obscuring ownership boundaries.
evidence: `disk_fstab.go` re-exports behavior from `system/disk` (`ParseFstabLine`, `ReconcileFstabLines`, `ReadFstabLines`, `WriteFstabLines`) via aliases/wrappers with no additional policy.
`disk_health.go` similarly forwards SMART/disk operations to `system/storage` (`SmartResultsSnapshot`, `SetSmartResults`, `ListHardDisks`, `SmartCheckAllDrives`).
`disk_fstab_test.go` is only a smoke placeholder, indicating the parent-layer files are largely structural shims rather than a validated facade contract.
findings.suggestion: Choose one boundary: either expose disk behavior directly from `system/disk` and `system/storage` (remove shims), or define a cohesive, policy-bearing `system` facade with explicit domain semantics and real contract tests.

findings.summary: Ship workflow package includes generic utility and persistence plumbing not aligned with lifecycle ownership.
evidence: `doc.go` describes `shipworkflow` as lifecycle/transition coordination, but `lifecycle.go` includes generic polling infrastructure (`PollWithTimeout`) reusable outside ship workflows.
`PersistUrbitConfig` is a thin forwarder around injected persistence function, placing config mutation plumbing in lifecycle package rather than at boundary-specific composition points.
`lifecycle_test.go` is only a placeholder smoke test, so package-level responsibility drift is not constrained by behavior-focused tests.
findings.suggestion: Keep `shipworkflow` focused on lifecycle orchestration: move generic polling to a shared runtime utility package and move config persistence adapters closer to config/integration seams; retain only ship-domain operations in this package.

findings.summary: Contract identity is defined through layered constants across modules, blurring the canonical boundary.
evidence: `actionids.go` defines raw namespace/action tokens; `protocol_contracts.go` redefines typed wrappers and recomposes IDs from those wrappers plus also exports legacy aliases.
Governance-defining metadata is separately embedded per contract family file, so boundary contracts are not represented through one strongly-typed central registry.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a single canonical contract registry package (typed owner enum, namespace/action binding, compatibility metadata) and make `actionids` and family-specific files consume generated/derived views from that registry; keep legacy aliases in a dedicated compatibility layer.

findings.summary: Declared governance/compatibility policy relies on conventions instead of structural enforcement.
evidence: README positions governance and quality gates as architecture contracts, but code-level policy fields (`OwnerModule`, `SinceVersion`, descriptive strings) are plain literals with no shared typed policy object.
Compatibility aliases are co-located with canonical contracts in `protocol_contracts.go`, coupling long-term migration concerns to day-to-day domain declarations.
findings.suggestion: Promote governance policy to first-class typed constructs (owner/module enum, version type/validator, compatibility declaration type) and split canonical declarations from compatibility aliases into separate files/modules with explicit lifecycle rules.

findings.summary: Tests validate local mechanics but miss policy-level guarantees for governance contracts.
evidence: Current tests focus on fixture validity and helper validation branches; they do not assert that every declared contract complies with governance policy constraints across families.
No test in this batch enforces that compatibility aliases remain intentionally mapped and non-divergent as canonical IDs evolve.
README’s quality-gate narrative implies contract-governance confidence, but there is no direct policy test proving docs-vs-runtime alignment for these governance-critical fields.
findings.suggestion: Add policy-level tests that iterate all contract catalogs and assert owner whitelist, version format/ordering, alias->canonical mapping invariants, and family-level governance rules; keep these tests as explicit quality-gate targets.

findings.summary: Docker event loops can spin on closed channels and process zero-value events indefinitely
evidence: In dockerListenerWithRuntime, `event := <-messages` and `err := <-errs` are read without `ok` checks (lines 183 and 196).
When either channel closes, select can keep choosing the closed case immediately; `err == nil` branch currently `continue`s (lines 197-202), enabling hot-loop behavior.
Existing tests in docker_test.go focus on transition workflows and do not assert channel-close behavior for listener loops.
findings.suggestion: Handle channel closure explicitly (`event, ok := <-messages`, `err, ok := <-errs`) and return/teardown when a stream closes unexpectedly; add a listener test that closes one channel and asserts the goroutine exits cleanly.

findings.summary: PersistUrbitConfig validates mutate callback but can panic on nil persist callback
evidence: PersistUrbitConfig checks `mutate == nil` (line 70) then directly calls `persistFn(patp, mutate)` (line 73) with no nil guard.
lifecycle_test.go is a placeholder smoke test only and does not cover nil-callback behavior.
findings.suggestion: Add `persistFn == nil` validation returning a clear error, and add targeted tests for both nil callback arguments.

findings.summary: PollWithTimeout can panic instead of returning an error for invalid interval input
evidence: PollWithTimeout calls `time.NewTicker(interval)` directly (line 77), which panics for interval <= 0.
Function is exported and reused by operations runtime (operations_runtime.go line 260), so helper contract currently depends on caller discipline rather than defensive validation.
findings.suggestion: Reject nonpositive intervals at function entry with a returned error (for example `fmt.Errorf("poll interval must be > 0")`) and keep ticker creation behind that guard.

findings.summary: Upload auth failures use inconsistent error context across endpoint-open and upload-validate paths
evidence: OpenUploadEndpoint returns coarse string errors for unauthorized states (`token mismatch`, `upload token id is not authorized` at lines 438-440).
validateUploadRequest in the same module wraps underlying authorization errors (`upload token validation failed: %w` line 731).
This creates two different diagnostics contracts for the same token-validation domain.
findings.suggestion: Centralize token authorization error mapping in a shared helper that preserves cause context consistently and returns stable category errors for both entrypoints.

findings.summary: Runtime state is partially bootstrapped at import time, making startup order and tests fragile
evidence: config package `init` sets runtime context immediately (config.go lines 230-232).
default runtime context includes docker directory resolution via defaults.DockerData, which probes host paths via `os.Stat` (defaults.go lines 113-129).
main package also mutates startup globals in `init` (`webContent`, `fileServer`, `initWebErr`; main.go lines 301-309).
findings.suggestion: Move import-time initialization into explicit bootstrap functions invoked from startup orchestrator/main, and keep package `init` side effects to deterministic constant wiring only.

findings.summary: Config event loop exports mixed error-handling contracts for equivalent startup actions
evidence: StartConfigEventLoop/StartConfigEventLoopWithRuntime return errors, but StartConfigEventLoopFromLegacyChannel and ConfChannel swallow startup errors and only log.
startup_orchestrator uses the error-returning entrypoint (`StartConfigEventLoopFn`), so API behavior differs depending on which exported symbol a caller picks.
findings.suggestion: Converge on one exported startup contract (prefer error-returning), keep legacy wrappers thin and deprecated but forwarding errors (or remove after migration window).
